NorthBridge Bank — Module 1, Lesson 1
How a Customer Request Travels Through NorthBridge Bank
Select a step to follow Rahul’s journey — outbound and return
STEP 1 OF 12 The Trigger Rahul taps “Check Balance” on his NorthBridge app

Rahul opens the NorthBridge app and taps Check Balance. Before the request travels anywhere, the app authenticates him — fingerprint or PIN. This check happens only on his phone. Then his request is encrypted into a sealed digital package. Nothing leaves the device until both steps are complete.

⚠Rahul’s device is entirely outside the bank’s control. Attackers target this layer through phishing emails, fake banking apps, malware, and credential theft. If Rahul’s device is compromised, an attacker can intercept his credentials before they ever leave the device — no firewall, no WAF, no encryption can stop that.
STEP 2 OF 12 The Internet The public road between Rahul and the bank

Rahul’s encrypted request enters the public internet — a network nobody owns or controls. It passes through ISP infrastructure and global DNS servers to reach NorthBridge Bank. Think of it as a sealed, locked courier package on a public postal road. Anyone can see the package moving. Nobody can open it.

⚠The internet is an untrusted, uncontrolled transit layer. Any packet travelling across it can be intercepted by an attacker who controls a router or ISP node along the path. Without proper encryption, every credential and every response would be readable by anyone on the same network path.
STEP 3 OF 12 — NETWORK PERIMETER The Firewall Guarding the boundary — creating the DMZ

The first device Rahul’s request meets is the Firewall. It sits at the network perimeter — the hard boundary between the untrusted public internet and the bank’s protected infrastructure. The Firewall filters every incoming request by IP address, port, and protocol. Anything that does not match the ruleset is blocked outright. The protected zone it guards behind it is called the DMZ — the De-Militarized Zone. The Firewall does not read the contents of a request. It only decides whether the request is allowed to knock on the door.

⚠Firewalls can be misconfigured. Overly permissive rules, open ports that should be closed, and poorly written IP allowlists are common weaknesses. An attacker who maps the bank’s perimeter can probe for gaps in the ruleset and slip traffic through on allowed ports.
STEP 4 OF 12 — INSIDE THE DMZ The WAF & Load Balancer Deep inspection and traffic distribution — inside the De-Militarized Zone

Once past the Firewall, the request enters the DMZ — the De-Militarized Zone. This is the buffer zone between the untrusted internet and the bank’s fully trusted internal network. Inside the DMZ, two more devices are waiting. The WAF (Web Application Firewall) inspects the request at the application level — detecting SQL injection, cross-site scripting, and other web-layer attacks that the Firewall cannot see. The Load Balancer then distributes incoming traffic evenly across multiple Web Servers so no single server is overwhelmed. These two devices are so critical to how NorthBridge stays safe that we go deep into both of them in the very next lesson.

⚠The WAF can be evaded using obfuscated payloads — encoded or split attack strings that bypass signature-based detection. The Load Balancer, if misconfigured, can be used to direct traffic to a specific backend server, concentrating attacks rather than distributing them. This layer is the last line of defence before the internal network.
STEP 5 OF 12 — INSIDE THE DMZ The Web Server The bank’s front door — routing and connection management

The Web Server is the first internal system Rahul’s request reaches after the security gateway. Think of it as the reception desk — it receives the request, handles the HTTPS connection, and routes it to the right place inside the bank. The Web Server does not decide whether Rahul is allowed to see his balance. That decision comes next.

⚠The Web Server is directly exposed to the public internet — every attacker’s first contact point. Vulnerabilities here — path traversal, misconfigured HTTPS, exposed admin endpoints — can give an attacker a foothold inside the bank’s infrastructure before any internal controls are reached.
STEP 6 OF 12 The Application Server Authentication and Authorisation — two separate questions

The Application Server is the brain of the operation. Two distinct questions are answered here. Authentication: who are you? Authorisation: what are you allowed to do? These are never the same question. Only after both checks pass does the App Server request the data from the Database.

⚠The Application Server handles authentication and authorisation for every customer account. A compromise here — through a session hijack, broken access control, or logic flaw — gives an attacker access to every customer record without ever touching the database directly. This is the highest-value target inside the bank’s infrastructure.
STEP 7 OF 12 — DEEPEST POINT The Database The source of truth — every account record stored here

The Database is where NorthBridge stores every account record, transaction, and balance. The App Server sends a precise query: “What is the current balance for this account?” The Database retrieves exactly that — nothing more. It does not decide what is legitimate. That decision was made upstream. This is the deepest point of the journey — from here, the data turns around and travels back.

⚠The Database is the crown jewel. It holds every account number, every transaction, every password hash for every NorthBridge customer. A successful SQL injection or misconfigured access control that reaches this layer exposes the complete financial records of the entire customer base. This is what every attacker is ultimately after.
↩ Return Path
STEP 8 OF 12 Return: Application Server Raw data formatted into a clean response

The Database sends Rahul’s raw balance record back to the Application Server. The App Server formats it into a clean response the banking app can display. Nothing extra is included — no other account data, no transaction history beyond what was requested. Minimal data, minimal exposure.

↩ Return Path
STEP 9 OF 12 Return: Web Server The formatted response reaches the front door

The Application Server passes the formatted response up to the Web Server. The Web Server wraps it in an HTTPS response, preparing it for the journey back across the public internet. The response now retraces the same path as the request — in reverse.

↩ Return Path
STEP 10 OF 12 Return: Security Gateway Passing back through the perimeter

The sealed response passes back through the security gateway — out of the bank’s trusted internal network. The gateway checks outbound traffic just as carefully as inbound. Security applies in both directions. Data exfiltration — data leaking outward — is a real attack vector.

↩ Return Path
STEP 11 OF 12 Return: The Internet The encrypted response travels back to Rahul

The encrypted response retraces the same road in reverse — from NorthBridge Bank back across the public internet to Rahul’s phone. The transit is fully encrypted. Anyone intercepting it on the road sees a sealed package they cannot open. The balance is protected all the way home.

STEP 12 OF 12 — CYCLE COMPLETE The Result Rahul sees his balance — round trip complete

Rahul’s app receives the encrypted response, decrypts it, and displays his balance. The entire round trip — from device to database and back through twelve steps across six layers — happened in under one second. One tap. Twelve steps. Every layer doing exactly its job. Rahul sees a number. You now know exactly what produced it.

Step 1 of 12
Key Principle
